seo.title: Building Prompt injection prevention with OWASP LLM Top 1...
seo.description: A step-by-step implementation guide for Prompt injection prevention with OWASP LLM Top 10 and Rebuff.

content.intro: This guide provides a structured approach to securing AI applications by addressing common vulnerabilities in prompt handling, API key management, data leakage, and compliance. Each step includes implementation details and real-world tool integration.
prerequisites: Existing AI application with LLM integration
Access to LLM provider APIs (e.g., OpenAI, Anthropic)
Vault or AWS KMS for secret management
Basic security auditing tools (e.g., Snyk, SonarQube)

steps.title: Conduct threat modeling for LLM interfaces
steps.explanation: Map attack vectors for user input channels. Identify where prompt injection, data exfiltration, and API key exposure risks exist. Use OWASP LLM Top 10 as a baseline.

code.content:
# Threat Model

## Attack Surface
- User input fields
- API request parameters
- System prompts

## Mitigations
- Input validation
- Prompt sanitization
- Access controls

pitfalls: Overlooking indirect input channels (e.g., file uploads)
Failing to update threat models during feature changes

steps.title: Implement prompt injection prevention
steps.explanation: Use framework-specific guardrails to isolate user input. Validate and sanitize prompts before LLM processing. Test with known injection patterns.

code.content:
from guardrails import Guard

prompt_guard = Guard().add("detect_prompt_injection")
secure_prompt = prompt_guard.validate(user_input)

steps.title: Secure LLM API key management
steps.explanation: Store credentials in secret management systems. Use IAM roles for cloud providers. Rotate keys regularly and monitor access logs.
pitfalls: Using shared credentials across multiple services

steps.title: Prevent data leakage in outputs
steps.explanation: Implement content redaction for sensitive information. Use LLM output filters to block prohibited patterns. Test with synthetic data samples.
pitfalls: Failing to test edge cases in output formatting

steps.title: Implement content moderation pipeline
steps.explanation: Integrate AI content filters to block harmful outputs. Use multi-layer validation (e.g., keyword matching + LLM-based classification). Regularly update banned word lists.

code.content:
from nemo_guardrails import Guard

moderation_guard = Guard().add("harmful_content_detection")
filtered_output = moderation_guard.validate(response_text)

pitfalls: Overlooking false positive rates in production

content.conclusion: Security for AI applications requires continuous validation of input handling, secret management, and output controls. Regularly update guardrails using OWASP LLM guidelines and audit implementations against compliance requirements.
